elements.sent-2411-001: The color-triplet partner of the Higgs doublet, called a $T$-particle, is a universal feature of Grand Unification.
elements.sent-2411-002: It has been shown some time ago that this particle can be accessible for direct production in collider experiments.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper we point out that the $T$-particle represents a simultaneous low-energy probe of baryon number violation as well as of the origin of the neutrino mass, linking the mediation of proton decay with oscillations of the neutron into a sterile neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-004: We point out a triple correlation between its collider signatures, proton decay measurements and the searches for the magnetic resonance disappearance of free neutrons in cold neutron experiments.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this way, the $T$-particle can provide a diversity of correlated experimental windows into Grand Unification.
elements.sent-2411-006: The goal of the present paper is to show that a light color-triplet partner of the Higgs doublet, predicted in \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp , can serve as a low energy window to Grand Unification via correlated signatures in different categories of experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Grand Unified Theories (GUTs) provide a symmetry-based framework for the idea of the unification of forces.
elements.sent-2411-008: The minimal structure is provided by the $SU(5)$ model of Georgi and Glashow~\cite{Georgi:1974sy which embeds the $G_{SM = SU(3)_c\times SU(2)_w \times U(1)_Y$ symmetry of the Standard Model (SM) in its compact covering group $SU(5)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This gauge symmetry is Higgsed (``broken") down to the SM group at the GUT scale, $M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The precise value of this scale depends on the low-energy particle content of the theory as well as on the threshold corrections from the physics at the scale $M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Already in the minimal case, the running of the coupling constants shows that the GUT scale is typically very high, $M_G \sim 10^{15-16}$GeV \cite{Georgi:1974yf .}
elements.sent-2411-012: This is also in accordance with the phenomenological bounds on proton decay mediated by the $X$ and $Y$ gauge bosons corresponding to the broken generators from $SU(5)/G_{SM}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: The universal feature of Grand Unification is the presence of the color-triplet partner of the SM Higgs doublet, $H$.
elements.sent-2411-014: We shall denote this particle by $T$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Due to the GUT symmetry, the $T$-particle is forced to participate in the $SU(5)$-invariant Yukawa couplings with quarks and leptons along with $H$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Correspondingly, in the limit of an unbroken $SU(5)$-symmetry, it mediates processes that violate baryon and lepton numbers.
elements.sent-2411-017: A particularly important process is proton decay which is subject to very stringent experimental bounds, with a lifetime longer than $\sim 10^{34}$yr.
elements.sent-2411-018: In order to accommodate such bounds, ordinarily, the $T$-particle is assumed to have a mass $\sim M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-019: This goes in contrast with the mass of its Higgs partner which is around $\sim 100$ GeV.
elements.sent-2411-020: This mass difference is traditionally referred to as the ``doublet-triplet splitting" problem.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, this is only a naturalness issue, rather than a consistency one: since the GUT symmetry is broken, the mass splitting can be easily arranged via fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-022: The extensions of the theory can allow for more natural solutions either based on symmetry (e.g., within the context of supersymmetric extensions of GUTs) or a cosmological relaxation of the Higgs mass \cite{Dvali:2004tma .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this work, we shall not enter into the naturalness issues of the mass-splitting scenarios which generate a large mass for the $T$-particle.
elements.sent-2411-024: From an observational point of view, a universal drawback of such scenarios is that they eliminate any chance of direct detection of the $T$-particle in collider experiments.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, an alternative realization of the theory exists in which such detection is possible.
elements.sent-2411-026: The idea, originally proposed in \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp , is that, instead of the masses, the splitting can take place among the Yukawa couplings of $H$ and $T$ particles.}
elements.sent-2411-027: As shown in this work, the breaking of GUT symmetry allows for a very strong relative suppression of the Yukawa couplings of the $T$-boson with ordinary quarks and leptons.
elements.sent-2411-028: This eliminates the necessity of a large mass splitting, thereby liberating the $T$-boson to be nearly degenerate with the SM Higgs.
elements.sent-2411-029: As a result, in this scenario the $T$-boson emerges as a light particle, with mass $m_T \sim$ TeV, but with suppressed baryon and lepton number violating couplings.
elements.sent-2411-030: This allows for the $T$-particle to be potentially accessible at colliders, without conflicting with other phenomenological bounds.
elements.sent-2411-031: This setup opens up the possibility of a direct detection of GUT's most universal feature in particle collisions as well as the correlation of its collider signatures with other observations.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the present paper, we point out some new implications for baryon and lepton number violating processes involving the neutrino sector.
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, we point out a triple correlation between the signatures in the following three classes of experiments: {\it 1) a direct production of the $T$-particle at high energy accelerators; {\it 2) the processes mediated by the $T$-boson in proton decay measurements; and {\it 3) the resonant transitions of a free neutron into the sterile neutrino, induced by the $T$-particle, potentially accessible in experiments with cold neutrons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Before proceeding, we would like to refer to two parallel complementary papers exploring implications of the $T$-particle for flavor physics \cite{Tonginpreparation and for cosmology \cite{Annainpreparation .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We would also like to point to various other implications of the $T$-particle with split couplings \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp, Dvali:1996hs, Gogoladze:1995sd, Dvali:1997qv, Berezhiani:1998hg, Bajc:2002bv, Rakshit:2003wj, Dorsner:2006ye, Belotsky:2008se, Berezhiani:2011uu, Dvali:2020uvd .}
elements.sent-2411-036: For simplicity, we shall work in $SU(5)$ theory with minimal field content and a single generation of fermions.
elements.sent-2411-037: In $SU(5)$ the SM fermions are arranged in a $\bar{5}$-dimensional and a 10-dimensional representation in the following way: \begin{equation}\bar{5}_F = \begin{pmatrix} d^c_r d^c_g d^c_b e^- -\nu \end{pmatrix}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}10_F = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 u^c_b -u^c_g -u_r -d_r -u^c_b 0 u^c_r -u_g -d_g u^c_g -u^c_r 0 -u_b -d_b u_r u_g u_b 0 e^+ d_r d_g d_b -e^+ 0 \end{pmatrix}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}10_F = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}  0 u^c_b -u^c_g -u_r -d_r -u^c_b 0 u^c_r -u_g -d_g u^c_g -u^c_r 0 -u_b -d_b u_r u_g u_b 0 e^+ d_r d_g d_b -e^+ 0 .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: Here, the superscript $c$ stands for denoting anti-particles, and $r,g,b$ are the three colors of QCD.
elements.sent-2411-039: All fermions are written in the left-handed basis.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, for generating the mass of the neutrino we introduce an $SU(5)$-singlet fermion $\nu^c$, also written in the left-handed basis.
elements.sent-2411-041: In the left-right basis, this would play the role of a right-handed (sterile) partner of the active left-handed neutrino $\nu$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The SM Higgs is embedded in a fundamental representation \begin{equation}5_H = \begin{pmatrix} T_r T_g T_b H^+ H^0 \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} where the SM Higgs doublet $H$ sits in the lower two components and a new triplet $T$ in the upper three.
elements.sent-2411-043: As usual, the breaking of the $SU(5)$-symmetry down to the SM group is achieved by a Higgs scalar in the adjoint representation which we denote by $24_H$.
elements.sent-2411-044: This field acquires a vacuum expectation value (VEV) \begin{equation}\label{VEV24} \langle 24_H \rangle \propto M_G \text{ diag} \Big(1,1,1,-\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{3}{2} \Big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: This VEV reduces the symmetry of the vacuum to $G_{SM}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The second stage of the symmetry-breaking is achieved by the VEV of the Higgs doublet $\langle H^0 \rangle = v$, living in the $5_H$-plet, $\langle 5_H \rangle=\begin{pmatrix 0 0 0 0 v \end{pmatrix}$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: This field generates masses of fermions via the $SU(5)$-invariant Yukawa couplings.
elements.sent-2411-048:
The minimal set of renormalizable couplings that can provide masses to all the fermions, including neutrinos, is given by the following structure:  egin{eqnarray}\Ll_{m fermi} = Y_d \: 5_H^* 10_F  ar{5}_F + Y_u \: 10_F 10_F 5_H + 
onumber + \, Y_{
u} \: 5_H  ar{5}_F 
u^c + m_R 
u^c
u^c \,,\end{eqnarray} where $Y_d, Y_u, Y_{
u}$ are the Yukawa couplings.
elements.sent-2411-049: The structure of $SU(5)$ and Lorentz indices is obvious and is not shown explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-050: The first row is responsible for the masses of the charged fermions, whereas the second row generates the mass of the neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-051: Depending on the values of $m_R$ and $Y_{\nu}$, the mass of $\nu$ can be either Dirac or Majorana type.
elements.sent-2411-053: Decomposing the above operators in terms of SM particles, we immediately discover that the couplings of $T$ and $H$ are related.
elements.sent-2411-054: In such a case, the mass of $T$ must be very large in order not to conflict with the experimental bounds on baryon and lepton number violating processes.
elements.sent-2411-055: This is the logic behind the standard assumption of the doublet-triplet mass splitting.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, notice that the masses of ordinary fermions generated from the above Yukawa couplings are not phenomenologically viable.
elements.sent-2411-057: In particular, there is a wrong prediction of equal masses for down quarks and electrons~\cite{Ellis:1979fg .}
elements.sent-2411-058: This rules out the simplest theory.
elements.sent-2411-059: To obtain viable masses, one necessarily needs to go beyond the minimal structure by adding higher-dimensional operators \cite{Ellis:1979fg .}
elements.sent-2411-061: This is necessary for communicating the message about the breaking of the $SU(5)$-symmetry to the Yukawa sector.
elements.sent-2411-062: In a similar spirit, the effective non-renormalizable operators can affect the unification of the gauge couplings \cite{Shafi:1983gz (for a recent analysis in this direction, see \cite{Senjanovic:2024uzn ).}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Now, the idea of \cite{Dvali:1992hc is that the high-dimensional operators with $24_H$ necessarily undo the $SU(5)$-relations among the couplings of $H$ and $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: As a result, in the low energy theory these couplings appear as independent parameters.
elements.sent-2411-065: In particular, the couplings of the color-triplet $T$ with the fermions can be arbitrarily suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-066: This liberates the mass of the $T$-particle, allowing it to be within the range of direct experimental accessibility at colliders.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, to the lowest order in $24_H$, there exist the following operators that correct the structure of the Yukawa couplings, \begin{equation}\label{eq:5dimoperators} \begin{split} \Ll_{\text{5-dim}} \supset \, \frac{g_1}{\Lambda} 5_H^* 10_F 24_H \bar{5}_F + \frac{g_2}{\Lambda} 5_H^* 24_H 10 _F \bar{5}_F + + \, \frac{g_3}{\Lambda} 10_F 10_F 24_H 5_H + \frac{g_4}{\Lambda} 24_H 10_F 10_F 5_H + + \, \frac{g_5}{\Lambda} 5_H 24_H \bar{5}_F \nu^c \,, \end{split}\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ is a cutoff scale and the $g_i$-s $(i=1,2,...5)$ are numerical coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-068: It is clear that upon inserting the VEV of the $24_H$ (\ref{VEV24 ) in the above operators, we get the effective low energy theory in which the $SU(5)$ relations among the Yukawa couplings are completely shattered.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For example, already the addition of the $g_2$-operator splits the $SU(5)$ invariant coupling $5_H^* 10_F \bar{5 _F$ between $T$ and $H$ as $Y_d + g_2 \frac{M_G {\Lambda}$ and $Y_d - g_2 \frac{3M_G {2\Lambda}$ respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This shows that the effective coupling of the $T$-particle can suppress proton decay to the observable level while accommodating the required Yukawa couplings of the Higgs.
elements.sent-2411-072: In general, one has to include all possible higher-order operators.
elements.sent-2411-073: Their precise structure depends on the cutoff $\Lambda$ and the specifics of the fundamental theory operating above it.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, there are some general consistency arguments indicating that the cutoff cannot be arbitrarily far from $M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-075: In particular, in any theory propagating $N$ particle species, gravity imposes a universal non-perturbative upper bound on the cutoff scale given by $\Lambda < M_P/\sqrt{N}$, where $M_P$ is the Planck mass~\cite{Dvali:2007hz, Dvali:2007wp}\footnote{This bound originates from the consistency of black hole physics and is fully non-perturbative in nature.}
elements.sent-2411-076: It is therefore exact and cannot be removed by any resummation of the series.
elements.sent-2411-077: The perturbative arguments supporting it can also be given (e.g., see \cite{Dvali:2001gx, Veneziano:2001ah ).. .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Notice that already within the SM, $N \sim 100$, and in GUTs it is naturally even higher.
elements.sent-2411-079: This brings the upper bound on $\Lambda$ at least an order of magnitude below the Planck scale.
elements.sent-2411-080: This fact is important as it tells us that, already from pure gravity, the corrections to the low-energy couplings cannot be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-081: In practice, $\Lambda$ can be even lower than the species scale and not far from the GUT scale.
elements.sent-2411-082: In particular, the operators of the type (\ref{eq:5dimoperators ) can be obtained from renormalizable interactions after integrating out multiplets with masses around $M_G$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The explicit realizations of such scenarios can be found in~\cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp .}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, specifying the structure of the high-energy theory is beyond the scope of the present paper.
elements.sent-2411-085: Instead, we shall treat the Yukawa couplings of the $T$-particle as free parameters of the low energy theory, and study certain interesting ranges of the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, through high-dimensional operators of the type (\ref{eq:5dimoperators ), the VEV of $24_H$ splits them arbitrarily.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The couplings of the doublet are fixed by the Higgs VEV ($v$) and the masses of corresponding fermions in the usual way: $Y_d' = m_d/v, Y_e'= m_e/v, Y_u' = m_u/v$.
elements.sent-2411-089: The analogous relation for the neutrino coupling/mass depends on the value of $m_R$ and will be discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-090: The couplings of the triplet shall be constrained by various processes with baryon and lepton number violation.
elements.sent-2411-091: The exchange of the $T$-particle can mediate baryon and lepton number violating processes.
elements.sent-2411-092: Let us first consider the processes of baryon (proton or neutron) decay.
elements.sent-2411-093: Such decays require an exchange that on one hand converts a quark into a quark and on the other hand a quark into a lepton.
elements.sent-2411-094: Correspondingly, the amplitudes of such processes are governed by the different cross products of the couplings of the type $T$-quark-quark ($g_{Tud , g_{TQQ}$) and $T$-quark-lepton ($g_{Tue , g_{TQL , g_{Td\nu}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-095:
For a clearer visualization, it is useful to write down the set of corresponding baryon and lepton number violating effective four-fermion operators obtained by the tree-level exchanges of the $T$-particle,  egin{equation}\label{4fermi}  egin{split} \Ll_{eff} \supset \:  rac{g_{TQQ}g_{Tue}}{m_T^2} \: Q Q u^{c*}e^{c*} \; + \;  rac{g_{Tud}g_{Tue}}{m^2_T} \: u^cd^c u^ce^c \; + + \,  rac{g_{Tud}g_{Td
u}}{m_T^2} \: u^c d^c d^c 
u^c \; +  rac{g_{TQQ}g_{TQL}}{m_T^2} \: Q Q Q L \; + + \,  rac{g_{Tud}g_{TQL}}{m^2_T} \: u^c d^c \, Q^*L^* \; + \;  rac{g_{TQQ}g_{Td 
u}}{m_T^2} \: Q Q d^{c*}
u^{c*} \,, \end{split}\end{equation} where $m_T$ is the mass of the $T$-particle and the star stands for conjugation.
elements.sent-2411-096: These operators can mediate proton and neutron decay in various channels.
elements.sent-2411-097: For example, consider a proton decay via the channel $p \rightarrow e^+ \pi^0$.
elements.sent-2411-098: A representative exchange diagram is given in \autoref{fig:protondecay .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Taking the mass of the triplet around its current collider bound, $m_T \sim$ TeV, the experimental bound on the proton lifetime, $\tau_p > 10^{34}$yr~\cite{Super-Kamiokande:2020wjk , implies the following constraint on the cross products of $T$-quark-quark and $T$-quark-lepton couplings, \begin{equation}\label{gg} {\rm (T-quark-quark) \cdot (T-quark-lepton)} < 10^{-26}\,,\end{equation} where the product runs over $(g_{Tud \cdot g_{Tue ), (g_{TQQ \cdot g_{TQL ), (g_{Tud \cdot g_{TQL ), (g_{TQQ \cdot g_{Tue )$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.384\textwidth]{protondecay.pdf} \caption{An example of a proton decay process mediated via the $T$-particle.} \label{fig:protondecay}
elements.sent-2411-100: Other processes of the proton decay into a charged lepton, such as $p \rightarrow e^+ \gamma$, give weaker bounds.
elements.sent-2411-101: Likewise, the above operators generate the process of neutron decay into a charged lepton $n \rightarrow e^+ \pi^-$.
elements.sent-2411-102: These are within experimental bounds provided the couplings satisfy the proton decay constraints (\ref{gg ).}
elements.sent-2411-103: We now turn to the processes that involve the neutrino sector.
elements.sent-2411-104: For this let us first identify the neutrino mass eigenstates.

elements.sent-2411-106: For a non-zero Majorana mass, $m_R$, the $\nu^c$ entering this coupling is not a mass eigenstate.
elements.sent-2411-107: Instead, it is a superposition of the mass eigenstates $\nu^{c \prime}$ and $\nu'$ of the sterile and active neutrino flavors.
elements.sent-2411-110: Next let us turn to the couplings of the $T$-particle that can mediate proton decay with a neutrino in the final state: \begin{equation}\label{TProton} \begin{split} g_{TQQ} \: T \, Q Q \; + g_{TQL} \: T^*\, Q L \; + + g_{Tud} \: T^* \, u^c d^c \; + g_{Td\nu} \: T \, d^c \nu^c . \end{split}\end{equation} The corresponding four-fermion operators obtained by $T$-exchange come from the second and third rows of (\ref{4fermi ).}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Neutron oscillations into the sterile neutrino
elements.sent-2411-127: We now wish to point out that the light $T$-particle can induce the interesting process of neutron oscillations into the sterile neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-128: This is possible if $m_R$ is close to the mass of the neutron $m_n$.
elements.sent-2411-129: The triplet exchange generates a mixing mass term between $n$ and $\nu^{c \prime}$, which we shall denote by $m_{n\nu^c}$.
elements.sent-2411-130: This mixing term induces the oscillations of a free neutron into the sterile neutrino with the period $\tau_{n\nu^c = m_{n\nu^c ^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The remarkable thing is that the transition process is correlated with the process of proton decay in the channel $p \rightarrow \pi^{+ \nu'$, essentially with no free parameters involved.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Indeed, all processes leading to this decay have counterparts that generate the mixing term $m_{n\nu^c}$.
elements.sent-2411-133: For example, a pair of corresponding representative diagrams is depicted in \autoref{fig:bothsteileneutrinoosc .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{sterileneutrinoosc.pdf} \caption{via $g_{Td \nu}$} \label{fig:sterileneutrinoosc} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{sterileneutrinoosc2.pdf} \caption{via $g_{TQL}$} \label{fig:sterileneutrinoosc2} \end{subfigure} \caption{Examples of diagrams mixing neutron with sterile neutrino via different couplings. } \label{fig:bothsteileneutrinoosc}
elements.sent-2411-134: The induced oscillation periods are in one-to-one correspondence with the expressions for the proton lifetimes.
elements.sent-2411-135: The difference is that the processes that go through the coupling $g_{TQL}$ exhibit a relative enhancement by a factor $\sqrt{\frac{m_R {m_{\nu }$ in neutron oscillation period versus the proton lifetime, as compared to the processes that go via $g_{Td\nu}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: In both expressions we took $m_R = m_n = m_p$.
elements.sent-2411-138: It is clear that for $\tau_p'$ and $\tau_p$ at the current experimental bound on the proton lifetime, the expression (\ref{tauR ) gives a shorter oscillation time.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Taking $\tau_p \sim 10^{32}$yr~\cite{Super-Kamiokande:2013rwg , we get for the oscillation period of a free neutron into the sterile neutrino $\tau_{n\nu^c \sim 10^{2 -10^{3}$s.}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: This time-scale is within the range of interest for the experiments that measure the disappearance of cold neutrons into hidden species \cite{nEDM:2020ekj,Ban:2023cja .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Both experiments are set up for testing the transition of a neutron into a nearly degenerate fermion.
elements.sent-2411-142: Therefore, they are ready-made for probing the proposed transition of the neutron into the sterile neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-143: These experiments place the ultracold neutrons in a magnetic field $B$ which changes the energy of the neutron proportional to its magnetic moment $\mu_n$, $\Delta E = \mu_n B$.
elements.sent-2411-144: When the energies of the neutron and the partner particle are nearly degenerate, a resonance increase in the neutron disappearance probability takes place.
elements.sent-2411-145: Ban et al.~\cite{Ban:2023cja performed a scanning of the magnetic field in steps of $\Delta B=3 \mu$T within the range of $50 - 1100 \mu$T.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Correspondingly, they were able to put a bound on the neutron disappearance time $\tau_{n n' > 1$s for a mass splitting $2-69$peV.}
elements.sent-2411-147: In the present context this bound translates as the corresponding constraint on $\tau_{n\nu^c}$ for the ``resonant" regime $m_n + \Delta E \simeq m_R$.
elements.sent-2411-148: If the splitting between $m_n$ and $m_R$ is significant, the transitions of the free neutron into $\nu^c$ are suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, for certain values $m_n > m_R$, the mass $m_R$ can come in resonance with some energy levels $E_n$ of a bound neutron inside the nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-150: In this case, the bounds on the couplings of $T$ become more stringent in order to accommodate the neutron disappearance bounds from the nuclei, which gives the time-scale $> 10^{30}$yr \cite{SNO:2022trz,KamLAND:2005pen .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Notice that for having a resonant transition, it is sufficient that the difference between $m_R$ and the neutron energy level $E_n$ is within the binding energy of the neutron $\Delta E_n \equiv m_n - E_n$ which is $\Delta E_n \sim$ MeV.
elements.sent-2411-152: The reason is that in such a case the neutron can transition into a sterile neutrino with a momentum $\vec{p}$ such that $E_n = \sqrt{m_R^2 + |\vec{p |^2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-153: This is because, for $|\vec{p | < \sqrt{\Delta E_n m_R}$ the momentum can be absorbed by the recoil of the nucleus.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Since the would-be oscillation period is much longer than the nuclear de-excitation time as well as the escape time of $\nu^c$, the inverse transition is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-155: Correspondingly, the outcome of the process will be a transition of the ``host" atom into an isotope with fewer neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-156: This transition will be accompanied by the emission of photons of nuclear energy.
elements.sent-2411-157: The non-observation of this effect puts the bound on the disappearance time $\sim 10^{30}$yr \cite{SNO:2022trz,KamLAND:2005pen .}

u^c$ transition for a nuclear neutron satisfies this bound, the corresponding two-body decays of the neutron and the proton, such as $p ightarrow \pi^{+ 
u$ and $n ightarrow \pi^{0 
u$, are well below the experimental limits.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: To summarize, for various resonant values of $m_R$ with neutron energy levels, the $T$-particle can mediate transitions between the neutron and $\nu^c$ for a free as well as for a bound neutron.
elements.sent-2411-160: This provides a new set of low energy probes of Grand Unification linked with the neutrino mass.
elements.sent-2411-161: In principle, the resonant values can be scanned by the external magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-162: One can say that in this setup the neutron energy levels provide a spectroscopy of the sterile neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-163: Of course, in this setup we treat the mass of the sterile neutrino as a parameter of the effective theory and derive its observable consequences.
elements.sent-2411-164: The exciting feature is that it gives a chance to probe GUT physics in low energy measurements with some spectacular signatures and correlate it with other observations.
elements.sent-2411-165: Since in the above parameter space $\nu^c$ is a Majorana fermion, its mixing with the neutron automatically induces a mixing between the neutron and the anti-neutron, see \autoref{fig:neutron antineutron .}
elements.sent-2411-166: The corresponding transition time for a free neutron is \begin{equation}\label{tnnbar} \tau_{n\bar{n}} \sim \tau_{n \nu^c}^2 m_n \sim 10^{30} {\rm s} \,,\end{equation} which is very far from the latest experimental limits, $\tau_{n\bar{n > 0.86 \times 10^{8}$s \cite{Baldo-Ceolin:1994hzw .}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: For a neutron that is in a bound state inside a nucleus, taking into account the additional suppression due to a level splitting between $n$ and $\bar{n}$, this gives a transition time $\tau_{n\bar{n ^{(nucl) \sim 10^{75}$yr which by many orders of magnitude exceeds the current experimental bound $\tau_{n\bar{n ^{(nucl) \sim 3.6 \times 10^{32}$yr \cite{Super-Kamiokande:2020bov .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Thus, for the ``resonant" regime $m_n \simeq m_R$, the dominant process allowed by the proton stability constraint is the oscillation between a neutron and a sterile neutrino \footnote{The correlation with proton decay is an important constraint on $n-\bar{n}$ versus $n-\nu^c$ transitions exhibited by the present GUT framework.}
elements.sent-2411-169: This goes in sharp difference with alternative extensions of the SM such as the $n-\bar{n}$ oscillations via mirror copies of the neutron \cite{Berezhiani:2020vbe where such correlations are absent.. .}
elements.sent-2411-170: In general, in this regime the $n-\bar{n}$ oscillation period $\tau_{n\bar{n }$ is additionally enhanced by the factor $\tau_{n\nu^c m_n$ relative to $\tau_{n\nu^c}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: This is because the $n-\bar{n}$ transition has to go via an intermediate $\nu^c$ state.

elements.sent-2411-172: Early discussions of its production mechanisms and collider signatures were given in \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp, Barbieri:1992yy, Dvali:1997qv, Belotsky:2008se, Cheung:2002uz .}
elements.sent-2411-173: More recent studies of bounds on new colored particles in the light of LHC data ~\cite{ATLAS:2022pib,ATLAS:2021mdj,ATLAS:2019gqq,CMS:2017kku,CMS:2016kce,ATLAS:2016onr set the lower bound on the mass of the $T$-particle around TeV.}
elements.sent-2411-174: For this mass, the correlations between various baryon number violating processes were discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-175: These processes constrain the lifetime of $T$, which is important for its LHC phenomenology.
elements.sent-2411-176: For example, assuming that the dominant couplings are given by the second row of (\ref{TProton ), the $T$-triplet can decay either into an up and a down quark or into a down quark and a sterile neutrino.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The dominance of the channel depends on the relative strength of the couplings, $g_{Tud}$ versus $g_{Td \nu}$.
elements.sent-2411-178: In the simplest case, in which the couplings of the doublet and triplet to the sterile neutrino are given by the $SU(5)$-relation, we can estimate, \begin{equation}g_{Td \nu} \sim \frac{\sqrt{m_\nu m_R}}{v} \sim 10^{-8}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-179: The $T$-quark-quark coupling is then constrained by the proton decay as, \begin{equation}g_{Tud} < 10^{-12} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-180: The corresponding lifetime of the $T$-particle is \begin{equation}\tau_T \sim 10^{-11} {\rm s} \sim 1 {\rm mm}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-181: Since this time-scale is much longer than the QCD length, the $T$-triplet will have plenty of time to hadronize and to form a color-singlet state.
elements.sent-2411-182: In particular, it can form a ``$T$-baryon" state with two fermions of the same color ($Tqq, T^*q^cq^c$), or a ``$T$-meson" state with a fermion of an anti-color ($Tq^c, T^*q$).
elements.sent-2411-183: Notice that, since the $T$-particle is a scalar, with such a definition, the $T$-baryon is a boson, whereas $T$-meson is a fermion.
elements.sent-2411-184: Assuming that upon its production the $T$-particle is moderately relativistic, the hadronized $T$-particle will decay via a displaced vertex.
elements.sent-2411-185: Depending on the precise values of the parameters, the decay can take place within the detector.
elements.sent-2411-186: For example, for a $T$-meson the decay products can be an ordinary QCD meson and a sterile neutrino, e.g., \begin{equation}(Td^c) \rightarrow \pi^0 + \nu^c \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-187: Analogously, for a $T$-baryon the decay can take place into an ordinary baryon and a sterile neutrino, e.g., \begin{equation}(T^*u^cd^c) \rightarrow \bar{n} + \nu^c \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Of course, for more suppressed values of its couplings, the decay can take place outside of the detector, in which case the $T$-hadron will appear as a stable particle.
elements.sent-2411-189: The various parameter regimes require more precision studies.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, the general message is that for the light GUT color-triplet scenario \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp , the searches for proton decay on one hand, and the cold neutron oscillations/disappearance experiments on the other hand become correlated with each other as well as with the production of a long-lived $T$-hadron states in colliders (see \autoref{fig:correlations ).}}
elements.sent-2411-191: The color-triplet partner of the Higgs doublet is a model-independent property of any GUT.
elements.sent-2411-192: Therefore, an experimental detection of the $T$-boson would be direct evidence of Grand Unification.
elements.sent-2411-193: As originally pointed out in \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp , the $T$-particle can be part of the low energy spectrum of the theory with the mass potentially accessible at collider experiments, such as LHC.}
elements.sent-2411-194: The reason is that already the lowest order operators involving the VEV of $24_H$ are capable of suppressing the baryon and lepton number violating couplings of the $T$-boson to the levels compatible with the current experimental bounds.
elements.sent-2411-195: This opens up an avenue for simultaneous searches of the signatures of the $T$-particle at high energy colliders as well as in the experiments looking for baryon and lepton number violating low energy processes.
elements.sent-2411-196: In particular, in the present paper we have pointed out the potential correlations among the searches at three very different sets of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-197: The first one comes from the link between proton decay and the oscillations of a cold neutron into a sterile neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-198: Namely, the current bounds on the proton lifetime put the $T$-mediated neutron-to-sterile neutrino oscillation processes within the ballpark of sensitivity of cold neutron experiments \cite{Ban:2023cja, nEDM:2020ekj .}
elements.sent-2411-199: In general, due to its vanishing SM gauge charges, the neutron represents an interesting probe of feebly-coupled new physics.
elements.sent-2411-200: The early examples of such effects include the neutron transitions into its hidden copies \cite{Dvali:1999gf, Berezhiani:2005hv, Dvali:2009ne .}
elements.sent-2411-201: It was shown recently \cite{Dvali:2023zww that within the framework of large extra dimensions \cite{Arkani-Hamed:1998jmv , the ``disappearance" or oscillations of a cold neutron into the extra dimensions is a rather generic phenomenon.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Moreover, this effect can be linked with the extra-dimensional origin of the neutrino mass \cite{Arkani-Hamed:1998wuz, Dvali:1999cn .}
elements.sent-2411-203: The analysis of the present work shows that a similar phenomenon can take place in GUTs.
elements.sent-2411-204: Moreover, here too the cold neutron transition is linked with the origin of the neutrino mass.
elements.sent-2411-205: Namely, via $T$-exchange a neutron can oscillate into a sterile neutrino which generates the Majorana mass for the SM neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-206: Thus, the GUT framework with a light $T$-particle can correlate the $T$-mediated proton and neutron decay processes with the oscillations of cold neutrons into the sterile neutrino species.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this way, the cold neutron experiments can potentially turn out to be simultaneous probes of Grand Unification and of the neutrino mass.
elements.sent-2411-208: In addition, the parameter space predicting the signatures for both the above types of measurements, at the same time, is linked with the direct production of the $T$-particle at the LHC.
elements.sent-2411-209: Thus, the $T$-boson, which represents an intrinsic part of any GUT, can manifest itself in the correlated signatures in three different types of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-210: Although we have illustrated the effect within the minimal $SU(5)$ with a single generation of fermions, it is straightforward to see that it persists in the generalizations to higher GUTs such as $SO(10)$ or $SU(6)$, as well as, in their supersymmetric extensions which have been formulated previously \cite{Dvali:1992hc, Dvali:1995hp, Dvali:1996hs, Berezhiani:2011uu, Dvali:2020uvd .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Moreover, the inclusion of couplings of the $T$-particle with heavier generations of fermions provides additional baryon decay channels and correlated transitions of the neutron into the corresponding flavors of the sterile neutrinos.
